To concede
I take issue with the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it.
Let me play devil's advocate against my own finding for a moment, because you are going to do it anyway.
She conceded the sample without hesitating, and then she stood her ground on what it meant.
I defer to this council entirely on the research question. I would not defer to anyone on what a sixty-two percent silence means.
That is a fair premise to test, and it does not hold water on exactly one point.
While the sample is small, the pattern inside it is consistent, and it is consistent with the one number nobody disputes.
Granted, the focus groups are anecdotal. That said, they are the only place anyone has asked this faculty a question in four years.
Much as I would like to defend the response rate, I would rather replace it, with an instrument this council helps me build.
Even if we accept that the data is anecdotal, the silence of sixty-two percent of a faculty still requires an explanation.
Granted, the sample is small, and I would not ask this council to treat it as representative.
That said, the sixty-two percent who said nothing are not missing data. They are the finding.
So I am not asking you to accept the number. I am asking two of you to help me build the instrument that replaces it, in March.
Much as I would like to defend the thirty-eight percent, the honest answer is that I would rather replace it.

Before I take your questions, let me start from the common ground and then hand you the weakest part of my own work. The response rate is thirty-eight percent. In your field that would not survive a review, and I am not going to pretend otherwise in this room.
Then we agree on the arithmetic, at least. Thirty-eight percent. In my field, that is not a finding. That is a rumor with a spreadsheet attached to it.

Granted, and I concede it without any reservation at all. That said, I would ask you to look at the other number. Sixty-two percent of this faculty were asked a direct question about how it feels to work here, and chose not to answer it. Those people are not missing data, Dr. Prescott. They are the finding.

That is an interpretation, and it is not an unattractive one. But it rests on four focus groups, which are anecdotal, and on an instrument that is not peer-reviewed, built by somebody who has been in this country for six weeks. Let me play devil's advocate. Suppose this faculty is perfectly healthy and your survey was simply badly timed.

Six weeks, and I would not claim otherwise, which is precisely why I ran an assessment rather than arriving with a plan. I defer to this council entirely on the research question, and I would not take issue with a word you have said about rigor. But your premise does not hold water on one point, and it is the only one I need: a healthy faculty that is asked how it feels does not decline to answer at a rate of sixty-two percent. Much as I would like to defend the thirty-eight, I would rather replace it, with an instrument that two members of this council help me build, in March.

Then I will build it with you, and I will say this to the council. Every consultant who has presented in this room arrived certain, and I have never believed one of them. You are the first who told me where your own number was thin before I could. That has merit, and I am prepared to be persuaded by the March figure.
Granted, the sample is small, and I would not ask you to treat it as representative. That said, the sixty-two percent who said nothing are the finding.
While it is not peer-reviewed, it was never designed to be. A diagnostic and a study answer two different questions.
I defer to this council on the research question. I would not defer to anyone on what a sixty-two percent silence means.

Danielle, it is Claire. You present to the Academic Council on Thursday, and there is one thing I need you to understand before you walk in. Alan Prescott is going to take issue with your methodology. He will do it in the first four minutes, he will be perfectly polite about it, and he will say that thirty-eight percent is anecdotal. Now, here is what I have watched happen to three people before you. They hear a doctor question their evidence, and they fold. They say, you are absolutely right, I will redo the whole assessment. And the moment they say that, the room quietly decides that the culture work is optional, and we do not get it back for a year. So do not fold. And do not fight him either, because you will lose that, and you should lose it, because he has published on this and you have not. What you do instead is the only thing that works in that room. You concede the part of his point that is true, out loud and without flinching, and then you hold the part that is yours. The sample is small. Say so before he does. And then say what the thirty-eight percent actually is, because it is not a weak finding, Danielle, it is a finding about something else entirely, and you are the only person in that building who can see it.

Claire, it is Alan. You asked me for my honest expectations of Thursday, so here they are, and you are welcome to pass them on to her. I have read the assessment twice. The instrument is sound, the questions are well built, and the response rate is thirty-eight percent, which in my field would not survive a review. I intend to say so, and I would ask you not to treat that as hostility, because it is not. It is the only professional courtesy I have to offer somebody who is new. What I am genuinely trying to find out on Thursday is not whether her data is perfect. It is whether she knows where it is thin. Every consultant who has ever presented to this council has arrived certain, and certainty, in a room of researchers, reads as inexperience. So if she tells me that the number is small and then tells me why it is small and what it means anyway, I will listen to the rest of it with a great deal of interest. And if she simply agrees with everything I say in order to get out of the room, then I am afraid I will have learned something about her that I would rather not know in her first month.
